title: Deep Water by William Douglas
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Conquering fear through determination, willpower, and systematic effort. The lesson contrasts fear of death with fear itself.

summary:
This is an autobiographical account of how William Douglas conquered his lifelong fear of water. His phobia started at the age of three or four when a wave knocked him down at a California beach and buried him underwater. Though his father laughed, the terror stayed. Years later, at ten or eleven, he decided to learn swimming at the YMCA pool in Yakima. It was considered safe, with only two or three feet of water at the shallow end. One day when he was alone, an eighteen-year-old boy picked him up and tossed him into the deep end as a prank.

Douglas sank to the bottom. Though terrified, he planned to hit the bottom, push up, and float like a cork. But when he came up, he swallowed water and sank again. He tried twice more. The third time, he stopped trying. His legs felt paralyzed, his lungs ached, and blackness swept over him. When he woke up, he was lying beside the pool, vomiting. The terror never left him after that. He avoided water for years, and it ruined his fishing, boating, and swimming.

Finally, he decided to get an instructor. The instructor put a belt around him, attached a rope, and taught him piece by piece: how to exhale underwater, raise his nose, and kick his legs. It took months to build confidence. But small traces of fear remained, so Douglas tested himself in Lake Wentworth, Triggs Island, and Warm Lake. When he felt the terror leave him, he knew he had conquered it.

He concludes that there is peace in death, but terror only in the fear of death. The experience taught him the meaning of Roosevelt’s words: “All we have to fear is fear itself.”
